coredata.dc:title: Red emitting carbon dots for detection of endogenous glutathione with fluorescence enhancement and cell imaging
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Tongtong
Chen, Huilin
Song, Ziyan
Hou, Yujia
Xu, Yuan
Wang, Qin
Liu, Qiaoling
coredata.dc:description: Glutathione (GSH) as a key endogenous antioxidant plays the essential role in many bioprocesses, and the lack of GSH would result in a series of diseases. In order to develop a fluorescent indicator for monitoring the fluctuation of GSH and provide information for clinical diagnosis, the red fluorescence carbon dots containing double bonds (DB-CDs) were developed by one-pot hydrothermal process. Owing to photoinduced electron transfer (PET) process between the surface amine groups and the carbon core, the DB-CDs presented the weak fluorescence. Upon addition of GSH, PET process was inhibited by addition reaction between sulfhydryl group of GSH and double bonds, and the bright red fluorescence was exhibited with an emission maximum (λ fl max ) of 630 nm. A good linear relationship was exhibited in the range of 0.12–75 μM with the detection limit as low as 34.6 nM. Moreover, the cell imaging and the fast kinetic data all demonstrated that the DB-CDs could detect the endogenous GSH without interferences from metal ions and other amino acids, suggesting that the DB-CDs could be used as fluorescence probe for GSH detection in living systems.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Red emission
Caron dots
Glutathione
PET
Cell imaging

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Photostability and Influence of ionic strength

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Shanxi Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is financially supported by Natural Science Foundation of Shanxi ( 20210302123090 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of the DB-CDs (inset: HRTEM image). (b) The size distribution of the DB-CDs.

(a) Absorption spectra of the DB-CDs (0.02 mg/mL) with GSH (0–250 μM). The inset is the image of the DB-CDs (left) and complex of the DB-CDs-GSH (right) in pure water under daylight, respectively. (b) Linear relationship between the absorbance of the DB-CDs at 535 nm and the concentrations of GSH (0.2–110 μM).

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of the DB-CDs (0.02 mg/mL) upon addition of GSH (0–250 μM). The inset is the image of the DB-CDs in water before(left) and after(right) addition of GSH under UV light of 365 nm. (b) Linear relationship of the fluorescence intensity at 630 nm with respect to the concentrations of GSH (0.12–75 μM).

Relative fluorescence intensity of the DB-CDs (0.02 mg/mL) in pure water with different amino acids (a) and different metal ions and sulphur-containing anions (b) by the concentration of 250 μM. The excitation wavelength is 535 nm.

Confocal images of HCT116 cells. (a) Cells were incubated with the DB-CDs (0.50 mg/mL) for 1 h. (b) Cells were incubated beforehand with NEM (500 μM) for 30 min, further cultured with the DB-CDs (0.50 mg/mL) for another 1 h. (c) Cells were incubated beforehand with α-LPA (100 µM) for 1 h, further cultured with the DB-CDs (0.50 mg/mL) for another 1 h. (d) The relative fluorescence intensity in different groups. Scale bar: 10 µm.
Determination of GSH in real serum samples (n = 6).

head.ce:title: Red emitting carbon dots for detection of endogenous glutathione with fluorescence enhancement and cell imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Materials, Taiyuan Normal University, Shanxi 030619, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Materials, Taiyuan Normal University, Shanxi 030619, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Glutathione (GSH) as a key endogenous antioxidant plays the essential role in many bioprocesses, and the lack of GSH would result in a series of diseases. In order to develop a fluorescent indicator for monitoring the fluctuation of GSH and provide information for clinical diagnosis, the red fluorescence carbon dots containing double bonds (DB-CDs) were developed by one-pot hydrothermal process. Owing to photoinduced electron transfer (PET) process between the surface amine groups and the carbon core, the DB-CDs presented the weak fluorescence. Upon addition of GSH, PET process was inhibited by addition reaction between sulfhydryl group of GSH and double bonds, and the bright red fluorescence was exhibited with an emission maximum (λ fl max ) of 630 nm. A good linear relationship was exhibited in the range of 0.12–75 μM with the detection limit as low as 34.6 nM. Moreover, the cell imaging and the fast kinetic data all demonstrated that the DB-CDs could detect the endogenous GSH without interferences from metal ions and other amino acids, suggesting that the DB-CDs could be used as fluorescence probe for GSH detection in living systems.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Glutathione (GSH) as a non-protein biothiol is composed of cysteine (Cys), glycine (Gly) and glutamate (Glu) to form an abundant tripeptide in living cells [1]. Due to GSH as a key endogenous antioxidant, it is involved in many critical physiological processes such as reduction of oxidative stress to maintain redox balance, avoiding from oxidation and crosslinking of protein thiols, protecting cells to ward off oxidative damages from the reactive oxygen species (ROS) and free radical overload, fighting against ageing and inflammation [2,3], speedy metabolism of toxins and support of immune function [4,5]. In normal cells, the concentration of GSH is around 1–2 mM except in hepatocytes where GSH is exported and its concentration could reach as great as 10 mM [6]. The depletion of GSH is considered to be related to certain health problems, such as cancer [7,8], HIV, cystic fibrosis, age-related diseases [9], and neurodegenerative disorders (e.g., Parkinson's disease and Alzheimer's disease) [9,10]. Thus, it is urgent to explore a sensitive fluorescent probe for real-time measurement of GSH fluctuation in order to early diagnosis of related diseases and following treatment. To date, many fluorescent probes or sensors for GSH were reported, including organic fluorescent dyes [11], metal chelates [12,13], metal–organic frameworks (MOF) [14,15] and nanomaterials. Owing to photobleaching and poor water-solubility, the applications of organic fluorescent dyes, metal chelates and metal–organic frameworks (MOF) as fluorescent probes are limited. Nowadays, nanomaterials as fluorescent probes have been extensively applied for detection of GSH. These nanomaterials could be broadly classified into 5 types: nanocomposite, carbon dots-metal ion complex, carbon dots-fluorescent dye hybrid system, metal nanoclusters and carbon dots (CDs). Typically, the detection methods of nanomaterials for GSH are first through fluorescence quenching that induced by other analytes binding with nanomaterials, and then the fluorescence is recovery through leaving combination of the analytes-GSH by addition of GSH. Herein, GSH maybe acts as inhibitor of fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) between CDs and Au NPs [16], or as reductant to reduce MnO2 to Mn2+ [17,18], or also as metal chelating agent by the sulfhydryl of GSH [19,20].These methods for GSH detection are more tedious and need the help from other analytes, the recognition for GSH depends on the reduction rate or dissociation rate of complexes between nano-materials and different analytes, which greatly affect the detection sensitivity and monitoring rate for GSH. Now only several CDs and metal nanoclusters (NCs) have reported to directly measure GSH. However, metal nanoclusters (NCs) will introduce heavy metal ions into the body to induce cytotoxicity [21], which is not applicable to living systems. The CDs now take center stage due to their distinct advantages, such as small size and good monodisperse, anti-photobleaching, excellent biocompatibility and good chemical inertness [22,23]. However, owing to the inherent characteristics of CDs, most of them involve in excitation by ultraviolet light [24], which greatly restricts their application in the biomedical field by reason of the potential photodamage and interferences from autofluorescence of biological samples. The CDs with red/near-infrared emission are desirable for the further application in living systems due to their low background autofluorescence, minor damage to biological samples and good tissue penetration. At present only two reported CDs could directly measure GSH with orange-red emission [25,26], however, they displayed fluorescence quenching by addition of GSH. In order to improve the current features of CDs, the genuine red fluorescence CDs with enhanced emission have been designed and fabricated via a one-pot hydrothermal process by using neutral red, dicyandiamide and anhydrous potassium carbonate as carbon precursors. The as-synthesized double-bond-containing carbon dots (DB-CDs) exhibited a large Stokes shift of 95 nm with weak red fluorescence by the excitation of 535 nm, however, the bright red fluorescence was observed by addition of GSH, which meant photoinduced electron transfer (PET) mechanism was effectively inhibited by addition reaction, and there was no interference from other amino acids and metal ions in this process. Furthermore, the commendable imaging capability of the as-fabricated DB-CDs in living cells demonstrated the great potential applications in biomedicine field.

Dicyandiamide, neutral red, anhydrous potassium carbonate and the various amino acids were purchased from leyan Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Solvents (AR grade), the different metal salts and the sodium salts with sulphur-containing anions were purchased from commercial suppliers. HCT116 cells were purchased from Wuhan Pricella Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Wuhan, China). Ultrapure water was applied in the whole process of experiment (Heal Force, China). The instruments were employed and described in the Supporting Information.

The DB-CDs were designed and fabricated by one-pot hydrothermal strategy from neutral red, dicyandiamide and anhydrous potassium carbonate as carbon precursors. The approach to the DB-CDs was optimized and presented in Fig. S1-S3 (Supporting information). Briefly, neutral red (0.0772 g), dicyandiamide (0.0228 g) and anhydrous potassium carbonate (0.0246 g) were put into 10 ml ethanol by stirring at room temperature. Subsequently, the mixed solution was rapidly transferred into a Teflon autoclave and heated to 140 °C for 5 h. After that, the reaction mixture was concentrated by rotary vacuum evaporator, the chromatography was used with ethyl acetate: ethanol (40:1 by volume) as the eluent, then the DB-CDs were purified and concentrated again by rotary vacuum evaporator, gained 32 wt% yield by lyophilization. Be sure the successful preparation of carbon dots and eliminate the impact of neutral red, the UV–vis and fluorescence spectra were employed in different polar solvents between pure water and ethanol, and more details were presented in the next section of Optical properties. Furthermore, the structural characterizations of the DB-CDs by TEM and XRD analysis unambiguously determined the DB-CDs were synthesized.

The stock solution of the as-synthesized DB-CDs was prepared in ethanol (4.0 mg/mL), and the test solution was measured in pure water. The stock solutions of different amino acids were prepared in pure water (0.01 M). The solutions of different metal ions were got ready from their nitrates and chloride salts (0.01 M). The fluorescence quantum yield was calculated and used Rhodamine B (Φ = 0.31 in water) as reference.

The cell cytotoxicity assays were depicted in Supporting Information. Cell imaging was carried out and HCT116 cells were employed and cultured in DEME medium containing penicillin/streptomycin (1 %) and fetal bovine serum (10 %) in 5 % CO2 atmosphere at 37 °C for 48 h. Then cultured cells were washed and divided into three groups. One group was only incubated 0.5 mg/mL DB-CDs at 37 °C for 1 h; Another group was firstly treated with N-ethylmaleimide (NEM, 500 μM) at 37 °C for 30 min, and then incubated with 0.5 mg/mL DB-CDs for another hour; the last group was pretreated with α-LPA(α-lipoic acid, 100 μM) for 1 h, and further cultured with 0.5 mg/mL DB-CDs at 37 °C for another hour. Finally, PBS buffer (pH 7.4) was used to wash these cells, and the fluorescence images were performed with excitation at 561 nm, the emission wavelength at 600–650 nm.

The morphology and the size distribution of the DB-CDs were investigated by TEM, and the DB-CDs presented the nearly spherical with monodisperse distribution (Fig. 1 a), the average size was 2.64 nm (Fig. 1b). The HRTEM image exhibited the typical lattice spacing with lattice spacing of 0.16 nm along the (002) direction [27]. This crystalline structure was further validated by XRD analysis (Fig. S4), a broad peak at ca. 25.7°corresponded to the (002) diffraction plane of carbon-based materials [28].

The surface element states and functional groups of the DB-CDs were first surveyed by XPS. The XPS full spectrum presented the three major peaks that were centered at 284.2, 398.1, and 531.1 eV, which belonged to C 1s, N 1s and O 1s. The element contents of the DB-CDs were about C 78.39 %, N 14.92 % and O 6.69 %, respectively (Fig. S5a). The high-resolution XPS spectrum of C1s presented three peaks at 283.8, 284.6 and 286.6 eV that could be assigned to C=C/C–C, C–N and C-O/C≡N/ C=N groups (Fig. S5b) [29–33]. The N1s band emerged two peaks centered at 398.5 and 399.4 eV (Fig. S5c) that could be matched with C-NH2/C≡N and C-N/C=N groups [26,33,34]. O1s spectrum displayed two peaks centered at 531.0 and 532.8 eV (Fig. S5d) that could be ascribed to C=O and C–O groups [35]. These analyses suggested that a large number of double bonds and amino groups, as well as a small amount of carboxyl groups were coexisted on the surface of the as-prepared DB-CDs.

The XPS results were further affirmed by FT-IR spectrum (Fig. S6). The FT-IR spectrum exhibited the absorption bands at 3343 and 3232 cm−1 that were ascribed to the stretching vibration of N–H group [36]. The peaks at 2909 and 2362 cm−1 were assigned to the stretching vibration of C–H and C≡N/conjugated alkene. The peaks at 1606 and 1005 cm−1 could be regarded as the asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibration of C-N group [37]. The peak at 1502, 1436 and 1309 cm−1 originated from the stretching vibration of C=C, C=N and C-O groups [34]. The peaks at 1183 and 809 cm−1 belonged to the stretching vibration of OCO and bending vibration outside the plane of aromatic C–H [38]. The results of the FT-IR spectrum were in agreement with the XPS assignments, which further confirmed again there were a large number of double bonds and amino groups with a small amount of carboxyl groups on the surface of the DB-CDs.

The spectral properties of the DB-CDs have been surveyed in details. In order to determine the purity of the DB-CDs and eliminate interference of neutral red. The different polar solvents of pure water and ethanol were employed by UV–vis and fluorescence spectra. Since the polarity of water is greater than that of ethanol, the difference of absorption peaks was not significant with the DB-CDs at 535 nm and neutral red at 530 nm (Fig.S7). With the decrease of the polarity of the solvent, the maximum absorption peaks exhibited the obvious contrast in ethanol with the DB-CDs at 460 nm and neutral red at 537 nm, indicating that the absorption peak of the DB-CDs is greatly influenced by different polar solvents. In addition, the obvious color difference in ethanol could be seen between faint yellow for the DB-CDs and pale pink for neutral red under daylight (Fig.S8a). The fluorescence spectra similarly showed the obvious contrast in ethanol with the single peak at 580 nm for the DB-CDs and the double peaks at 500 and 600 nm for neutral red under the excitation of 460 nm. The distinguishable colors in ethanol were showed with yellow fluorescence for the DB-CDs and orange-red fluorescence for neutral red under UV light of 365 nm (Fig.S8b). Thus, the pure DB-CDs were obtained. Subsequently, the UV–vis spectra and fluorescence characteristics of the as-prepared DB-CDs were presented in pure water. A weak absorption peak at 535 nm was displayed (Fig. 2 ). Upon addition of GSH (0–250 μM), the absorbance obviously enhanced by 2.1fold in comparison with the free DB-CDs, suggesting the formation of DB-CDs-GSH complex. At the same time, the color changed from almost colorless to bright pink, the inset was the image of the DB-CDs before(left) and after(right) addition of GSH in pure water under daylight. It is noteworthy that the absorption peak remains unchanged, thus the increased absorbance might ascribe to the PET process [39,40]. The absorbance at 535 nm was linearly proportional to the concentration of GSH in the range of 0.2–110 μ M (Fig. 2b).

The fluorescence spectra of the as-synthesized DB-CDs were then discussed in detail at the different excitation wavelength (460–600 nm), the excitation-independent emission property was exhibited (Fig. S9), indicating the surface state of the DB-CDs was well-passivated with stable properties [41,42]. The free DB-CDs displayed the weak fluorescence emission with an emission maximum (λ fl max) of 630 nm upon the optimal excitation wavelength at 535 nm (Fig. 3 a), the fluorescence quantum yield (φ) is 0.15 (Eq. 1 in Supporting information). The weak fluorescence might assign to photoinduced electron transfer (PET) process. Generally, CDs consist of carbon core and chemical groups on the surface, carbon core is a conjugated π-domain by sp2 carbon structure analogous to the graphene, while the functional groups on the surface of CDs have the ability to regulate the electron transfer and further affect the fluorescence behavior of CDs. Herein, the amino groups on the surface of the DB-CDs were abundant, which is favor of the electron transfer from the surface amine groups to the carbon core [43,44], thus, the PET process was induced and led to the weak fluorescence. With the addition of GSH, the PET process was suppressed with the significant increase in fluorescence intensity by 2.8-fold (φ = 0.22) in comparison with the free DB-CDs, and good linearity relation was proved between the fluorescence intensity at 630 nm and GSH concentration in the range of 0.12–75 μM (Fig. 3b). The detection limit was 34.6 nM by the 3σ/k method [45,46], suggesting the as-synthesized DB-CDs not only have the large stokes shift of 95 nm with the emission of red light, but also could be employed as a fluorescence enhanced probe for GSH with very low detection limit.

In order to ascertain the interaction between as-fabricated DB-CDs and GSH, 1H NMR spectra were employed and depicted in Fig. S10. By comparison with the complex of the DB-CDs-GSH, the free DB-CDs showed the peculiar proton peaks at 7.20, 5.75 and 4.35 ppm (blue color) that might belong to the alkene, conjugated alkene and enamine [47–49]. With addition of GSH, the above three peaks disappeared and the new proton peaks emerged at 3.25, 3.10 and 1.93 ppm (red color) that could be assigned to the α- or β-position proton containing heteroatom structures similar to C-O and C-N, and the peak at 1.93 ppm also might be the proton of saturated aliphatic hydrocarbons from the addition reaction by GSH. The other new peaks at 8.47 and 8.22 ppm (yellow color) belonged to the different amino groups of GSH. All of these demonstrated that the active α-or β-unsaturated bonds of the DB-CDs went through the addition reaction by sulfhydryl group of GSH. Moreover, a visible up-field shift of the proton signals at 6.01–7.81and 3.02 ppm further confirmed the addition reaction by GSH. Thus, we speculated that the strong electron withdrawing ability of the carbon core polarized the α- or β-unsaturated bonds, and the polarized unsaturated bonds are in favor of the nucleophilic addition with soft electrophiles, such as GSH [50,51]. The experiment of the zeta potential proved it. The zeta potential of the DB-CDs was measured about −1.08 mV, which demonstrated the low protonation degree of amino groups induced by the strong electron withdrawing ability of the carbon core (Fig. S11). With addition of GSH, the zeta potential of the DB-CDs-GSH complex changed to 3.24 mV, suggesting that GSH was bonded to the unsaturated bonds and changed the charge distribution on the surface of the DB-CDs.

To further support the above speculation, the FT-IR spectra were required and depicted in Fig. S12. Comparation of the spectra before and after adding GSH, upon addition of GSH, the conjugated alkene at 2362 cm−1 disappeared (green color), carbon–carbon double bond and carbon–nitrogen double bond at 1502 and 1436 cm−1 all greatly weakened (purple color), while the C–N group at around 1606 cm−1 was still remarkable (yellow color), and C–H group at around 809 cm−1 presented two different peaks (pink color). Which implied the conjugated structure on the surface of the DB-CDs were broken by the addition reaction and formed the C-N group and C–H group. This conclusion was also supported by early studies [52–55]. Thus, the fluorescence enhancement of the as-synthesized DB-CDs by addition of GSH could be ascribed to the break of the conjugated systems by the addition reaction of GSH, which blocked the electronic transfer from the amine groups on the surface of the DB-CDs to the carbon core, resulting the suppression of the PET process and enhancement of the fluorescence.

The kinetics of the as-fabricated DB-CDs for GSH was discussed in detail. The time-dependent fluorescence response was recorded in pure water upon the excitation of 535 nm. As shown in Fig. S13a, the fluorescence intensity enhanced rapidly with time at the beginning, and then gradually attained its maximum after ∼20 min. The pseudo-first-order rate constant (K) of the reaction between the as-synthesized DB-CDs and GSH was obtained as 0.17 min−1 based on the following equation: ln (F max-F t) = ln F max – Kt (Fig. S13b), suggesting that the interaction between the DB-CDs and GSH was faster than many reported probes based on organic fluorescent dyes [56,57], implying the DB-CDs have the potential for real-time monitoring for GSH.

The specific selectivity of the as-prepared DB-CDs for GSH was assessed in detail by the different analytes including the various amino acids and metal ions upon excitation at 535 nm. As shown in Fig. 4 a, only GSH induced the obvious fluorescence enhancement, while the other amino acids, including sulfhydryl-containing cysteine (Cys) and homocysteine (Hcy), exhibited little response to the DB-CDs. The negligible effects of sulfhydryl-containing amino acids on the DB-CDs might be attributed to the disadvantage for Cys and Hcy to generate7-/8-membered ring heterocycles by intramolecular cyclization on the surface of the DB-CDs after the conjugate addition by sulfhydryl group [58,59], while GSH does not undergo the intramolecular cyclization, thus, the DB-CDs exhibited the high selectivity for GSH over Cys and Hcy. Moreover, the effects of the DB-CDs with the different metal ions were also investigated (Fig. 4b), the fluorescence intensities revealed a little decrease by addition of Ag+, Co2+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Al3+ and Cr3+, the other metal ions, such as K+, Na+, Hg2+, Hg2 2+, Ba2+, Cd2+, Sr2+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Zn2+ and sulphur-containing anions including HS-, SO3 2-, S2O3 2-, SO4 2-, all display the negligible effects on the DB-CDs. These results certified that the as-synthesized DB-CDs have the good selectivity for GSH.

The pH effects on the as-synthesized DB-CDs were evaluated in PBS buffer solution with the physiologically relevant pH (4.5–9.0). As presented in Fig. S14, the higher fluorescence intensity was exhibited in the acidic environment, and the fluorescence intensity gradually reduced with the rise of pH value, which may be attributed to the large number of amino groups and a small amount of carboxyl groups on the surface of the DB-CDs. With the decrease of pH from 7.0 to 5.0, the protonation degree of amino groups increases, which inhibited the electron transfer from the surface of the DB-CDs to the carbon core, thus the PET process is restrained with the fluorescence enhancement. On the other hand, with the increase of pH from 7.0 to 9.0, deprotonation occurred including not only amino groups, but also carboxyl groups, which facilitated the electron transfer from the surface functional groups to the carbon core, resulting the reinforced PET process and the decrease of fluorescence intensity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photostability and Influence of ionic strength
ce:section.ce:para: The photostability of the as-synthesized DB-CDs was investigated through survey of fluorescence intensity at 630 nm with continued exposure under ultraviolet light at 365 nm for 120 min, the fluorescence intensity was hardly changed. Continue to extend exposure time to 180 min, the fluorescence intensity kept almost the same, which illustrated that the DB-CDs have the fine photostability against photobleaching (Fig. S15). In addition, the negligible impacts were revealed on the effects of the DB-CDs in the solution with the different ionic strengths (0.0–2.0 mM KCl) (Fig. S16). All of which proposed that the DB-CDs are more appropriate to be used in physiological conditions.

To evaluate the application in real sample, the commercially available fetal bovine serum was employed and the detection capability of the DB-CDs for GSH was assessed by standard addition method. As depicted in Table 1 , the recoveries were in the range of 99.90 %–100.20 % with RSD of below 3 %, which strongly suggested the feasibility of the as-fabricated DB-CDs for GSH measurement in real sample analysis.

The further biological application was encouraged, HCT116 cells were used to assess the cytotoxicity of the DB-CDs by MTT assay. The high cell viability (>90 %) was presented after incubation with the as-synthesized DB-CDs (1.0 mg/mL) for 24 h (Fig.S17), suggesting that the DB-CDs had the good cell permeability and excellent biocompatibility. Subsequently, the potential of the DB-CDs for the imaging of endogenous GSH was estimated, and the HCT116 cells were divided into three groups. One of them was only incubated with the DB-CDs at 37 °C for 1 h as the control group, and then incubated cells were washed and taken into imaging with excitation at 561 nm and emission at 600–650 nm (Fig. 5 a). The weak fluorescence was emerged that could be attributed to the endogenous GSH. For fear of the interference from endogenous GSH, N-ethylmaleimide (NEM) was pre-treated to another group of cells at the concentration of 500 μM at 37 °C for 30 min, subsequently, further cultivated with the DB-CDs for another 1 h, and almost no fluorescence was found due to NEM as a thiol blocking reagent (Fig. 5b). Finally, the remaining cells were pre-incubated with α-lipoic acid (α-LPA) for 1 h, and then continued to incubate with the DB-CDs for another 1 h, the bright red fluorescence was presented owing to α-LPA as a well-known activator for GSH (Fig. 5c). The mean fluorescence intensities were measured and depicted in Fig. 5d. The results of cell images demonstrated that the DB-CDs are able to monitor the endogenous GSH fluctuation in living cells.

On the other hand, comparation and analyses were made between the DB-CDs and the reported two carbon dots that could monitor GSH directly [25,26], the decided advantages of the DB-CDs over the reported carbon dots have been presented in Table S1. Only the DB-CDs exhibited the large excitation and emission wavelengths with enhancement of genuine red fluorescence for GSH sensing, and potential real-time monitoring for GSH with rapid detection.

In summary, the genuine red-emitting DB-CDs have been developed based on one-pot hydrothermal method. The free DB-CDs exhibit the weak fluorescence in pure water due to PET process under the excitation of 535 nm. Upon addition of GSH, the bright red fluorescence is observed with the inhibition of PET process by the addition reaction between double bonds and GSH. Furthermore, the as-fabricated DB-CDs display a fast response to GSH with the low detection limit of 34.6 nM. Additionally, the cell imaging demonstrates that the DB-CDs could measure the endogenous GSH in living cells with the good cell permeability, implying the potential application of GSH detection in biomedical field.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is financially supported by Natural Science Foundation of Shanxi (20210302123090).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2025.125901.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

Nutrients

Front. Nutr.

J. Nutr.

Mol. Aspects Med.
